On this page

Specular Andreev Reflection in Graphene: From Dirac Fermions to Superconducting Proximity ​

The interface between a normal metal and a superconductor hosts one of the most elegant phenomena in condensed matter physics: Andreev reflection, where an incident electron is retro-reflected as a hole while a Cooper pair is injected into the superconductor. In ordinary metals this process is governed by non-relativistic Schrödinger electrons, and the reflected hole traces back along the incoming trajectory. Graphene changes this picture fundamentally. Its charge carriers are massless Dirac fermions governed by a linear dispersion, and when the Dirac equation is married to the Bogoliubov–de Gennes theory of superconductivity, the electron-hole conversion acquires new kinematic possibilities — most strikingly, a transition from retro-reflection to specular reflection as the chemical potential is tuned through the charge neutrality point.

1. Andreev Reflection: The Basic Mechanism ​

At a normal-metal–superconductor (NS) interface with subgap bias |ε|<Δ0, single-electron states cannot propagate into the superconductor. An incident electron from the normal side must therefore be reflected, but the superconducting gap demands that charge be transferred into the condensate in units of 2e. The microscopic process is:

  1. The incident electron approaches the interface.
  2. A second electron is pulled from the normal region to form a Cooper pair in the superconductor.
  3. The resulting vacancy in the normal region propagates backward as a hole.

Charge is conserved: the normal region loses net charge −e (one electron removed, one hole left behind), while the superconductor gains −2e (one Cooper pair). Particle number, however, is not a conserved quantity across the interface; what matters is the coherent conversion between electron and hole excitations.

In a conventional metal with parabolic dispersion, the reflected hole belongs to the same conduction band as the incident electron. Its group velocity points opposite to its wave vector, so the reflection is retro-grade: the hole retraces the incoming path. Graphene introduces two new ingredients that overturn this intuition.

2. Why Graphene Needs the Dirac Equation ​

Before writing down the Bogoliubov–de Gennes Hamiltonian, it is worth recalling why graphene is special. A free electron in vacuum obeys the Schrödinger equation with a parabolic dispersion E=p2/2m. Graphene's charge carriers do not.

Honeycomb Lattice and Sublattices ​

Graphene is a single atomic layer of carbon arranged in a honeycomb lattice. The lattice is not Bravais: it consists of two interpenetrating triangular sublattices, conventionally called A and B. Every A atom has three nearest neighbours, all on the B sublattice, and vice versa.

To make the geometry explicit, place an A atom at the origin. The three vectors connecting this A atom to its nearest B neighbours are

δ1=a(10),δ2=a(−1/23/2),δ3=a(−1/2−3/2),

where a≈1.42Å is the carbon-carbon bond length. The primitive lattice vectors of the underlying triangular Bravais lattice are

a1=a(3/23/2),a2=a(3/2−3/2).

Every lattice site can be written as R=n1a1+n2a2 for integers n1,n2; A sites sit at these points, while B sites sit at R+δ1.

From Atomic Orbitals to the Tight-Binding Hamiltonian ​

The electronic structure follows from the pz orbitals of carbon, which stick out of the plane and form π bonds with their three neighbours. Because the pz orbital is odd under reflection in the plane, it does not hybridize with the in-plane sp2 bonds; the π and σ sectors decouple.

In the tight-binding approximation one keeps only the dominant matrix element: the hopping amplitude t≈2.7eV between nearest-neighbour pz orbitals. The Hamiltonian is

H=−t∑⟨i,j⟩(|i⟩⟨j|+|j⟩⟨i|),

where the sum runs over all nearest-neighbour pairs ⟨i,j⟩ and |i⟩ denotes the pz orbital on site i.

Because the lattice has two atoms per unit cell, it is natural to introduce Bloch states for each sublattice separately:

|k,A⟩=1N∑Reik⋅R|R,A⟩,|k,B⟩=1N∑Reik⋅R|R,B⟩.

In this basis the Hamiltonian becomes a 2×2 matrix at each k:

H(k)=(0h(k)h∗(k)0),

where the off-diagonal element collects the three nearest-neighbour phase factors:

h(k)=−t∑j=13eik⋅δj=−t[eikxa+2e−ikxa/2cos(3kya2)].

Diagonalizing H(k) gives the two Bloch bands

E(k)=±|h(k)|=±t|∑j=13eik⋅δj|.

The plus sign is the conduction band (π∗), the minus sign the valence band (π). The bands touch whenever h(k)=0, which happens at six points on the edge of the hexagonal Brillouin zone. Only two of these are inequivalent; they are labelled K and K′ and are related by time reversal.

The Dirac Point ​

In the reciprocal lattice the primitive vectors are

b1=2π3a(1−3),b2=2π3a(13).

The two inequivalent corners of the first Brillouin zone are at

K=2π33a(02),K′=2π33a(31).

Expanding h(k) to linear order around K by setting k=K+q with |q|a≪1 gives

h(K+q)≈3ta2(qx+iqy)≡ℏvq+,

with v=3ta/2ℏ≈106m/s. The band energies are therefore

E(q)=±ℏv|q|=±ℏv|k−K|.

The dispersion is linear and gapless: the positive and negative branches form two cones touching at a single point. This is the Dirac point.

Linear dispersion means the effective mass vanishes. The low-energy excitations are not Schrödinger electrons; they are massless Dirac fermions. Their wave equation is the two-dimensional Dirac equation with zero rest mass:

H=ℏvp⋅σ=ℏv(pxσx+pyσy).

Here σ=(σx,σy) are Pauli matrices, but they do not act on real spin. They act on sublattice pseudospin: the two components of the spinor correspond to the amplitude on the A and B sublattices. The eigenstates are plane waves with definite chirality: the pseudospin is locked parallel or antiparallel to the momentum.

The two valleys K and K′ are described by the same Hamiltonian with opposite sign of py (or, equivalently, by exchanging σx→σx, σy→−σy). For the NS interface problem the valley index is largely a spectator; the physics is captured by a single-valley Dirac equation.

What Changes in an NS Junction? ​

For Andreev reflection the crucial difference between graphene and an ordinary metal is the fate of the hole. In a parabolic band the hole sits in the same band as the electron, merely at a different momentum. In graphene the situation is different because the conduction and valence bands touch linearly at the Dirac point. After Andreev reflection the hole carries energy EF−ε (the electron carried EF+ε). Whether this energy is positive or negative relative to the Dirac point determines which band the hole state belongs to:

  • Retro-reflection (EF>ε): the hole energy EF−ε lies above the Dirac point. The hole state is formally in the conduction band (positive-energy branch of the Dirac cone), but as a hole excitation — an absence of an electron — its group velocity points opposite to its wave vector. The reflected hole therefore retraces the incident electron trajectory.
  • Specular reflection (EF<ε): the hole energy EF−ε lies below the Dirac point. The hole state is in the valence band (negative-energy branch). There the group velocity of a hole points parallel to its wave vector, so the reflected hole exits at the same angle as the incident electron, on the same side of the normal — a mirror-like specular reflection.

The essential physics is that the sign of the hole energy relative to the Dirac point controls the relative orientation of its group velocity and wave vector. This single fact is the root of everything that follows.

3. The Dirac–Bogoliubov–de Gennes Equation ​

To incorporate superconductivity into the Dirac Hamiltonian, one couples electrons and holes through the pairing potential Δ and writes the four-component Dirac–Bogoliubov–de Gennes (DBdG) equation:

(p⋅σ−U−EFΔΔ∗U+EF−p⋅σ)(uv)=ε(uv).

Here u and v are two-component spinors describing the electron and hole amplitudes, EF is the Fermi energy in the normal region, and U is a potential shift that can be used to model doping.

Doping as a Potential Shift ​

In pristine graphene the valence and conduction bands touch at the Dirac point, and the Fermi level sits exactly at zero energy. Real samples are never perfectly intrinsic; some mechanism always pushes EF away from the Dirac point. The scalar term −U⋅I in the Hamiltonian captures this by rigidly shifting the entire band structure: U>0 pulls the bands downward so that EF lies in the conduction band (electron or n-type doping), while U<0 pushes them upward so that EF lies in the valence band (hole or p-type doping).

Experimentally this shift is achieved in several ways. A back-gate voltage across a dielectric layer electrostatically induces carriers and tunes EF continuously over hundreds of meV. Chemical adsorbates such as potassium atoms or NH3 molecules donate or accept electrons, producing fixed doping densities as large as 1013cm−2. The SiO2 substrate itself usually introduces a mild p-type background because of surface trapped charges. Encapsulation in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) suppresses this substrate-induced disorder and allows the sample to be tuned close to charge neutrality (U≈0) with high mobility.

In the NS junction model the superconducting electrode is taken to be heavily doped: we set U=−U0 with U0≫EF,ε,Δ0 in the region x<0. This guarantees a large Fermi surface in the superconductor, which simplifies the boundary-condition matching and reflects the experimental fact that metallic superconducting contacts are always strongly n-type. In the normal graphene region (x>0) we set U=0, using the normal region as the energy reference.

The crucial point is that the doping level in the normal region — controlled by EF relative to the Dirac point — determines whether the reflected hole lives in the conduction band or the valence band, and therefore whether Andreev reflection is retro-grade or specular.

The superconducting proximity effect in graphene is thus a marriage of relativistic kinematics (Dirac electrons) and Cooper-pair correlations (Bogoliubov quasiparticles). The cross-term Δ couples states that live in different valleys of the band structure — a feature absent in conventional metals.

4. Normal-Region States: Electrons and Holes ​

Setting Δ=0 and U=0 in the normal region, the DBdG equation decouples into two independent 2×2 Dirac equations.

Electron States ​

For electron states (v=0) the equation reads (p⋅σ−EF)u=εu.

From Two Components to Four Components ​

In the normal region the DBdG equation decouples: the electron sector (u) and the hole sector (v) satisfy independent 2×2 Dirac equations. An electron state therefore has v=0, and a hole state has u=0. In principle one could work entirely with two-component spinors and simply remember which sector is active.

However, the boundary condition at the NS interface requires continuity of the full four-component wave function (u,v)T. Matching at x=0 mixes electrons and holes through the superconducting pair potential Δ. It is therefore convenient to keep all four components explicit, padding the inactive sector with zeros. The electron eigen spinors written below are four-component objects with zeros in the lower two (hole) entries; the hole eigen spinors will have zeros in the upper two (electron) entries. This convention makes the boundary-condition matching in §7 transparent.

Plane-Wave Ansatz and Its Motivation ​

The NS interface is taken to lie in the y-z plane at x=0, so the Hamiltonian is translationally invariant along y. Momentum in the transverse direction is therefore conserved, and every eigenstate can be labelled by a definite wave number q. This justifies the factor eiqy.

In the longitudinal direction x the system is not uniform — the interface breaks translational symmetry — but away from x=0 both the normal and superconducting regions are themselves homogeneous. In each bulk region the wave function must therefore be a superposition of plane waves eikxx. The ansatz

u(x,y)=eiqyeikxxχ

encodes precisely this structure: eiqy fixes the conserved transverse momentum, eikxx describes propagation toward or away from the interface, and the two-component spinor χ carries the sublattice (pseudospin) degree of freedom.

Deriving the Dispersion and the Two kx Solutions ​

Substituting the ansatz into (p⋅σ−EF)u=εu gives

ℏv(kxσx+qσy)χ=(EF+ε)χ.

Writing out the Pauli matrices explicitly,

ℏv(0kx−iqkx+iq0)(χ1χ2)=(EF+ε)(χ1χ2).

This is a 2×2 eigenvalue problem. The characteristic equation is obtained by requiring the determinant to vanish:

det(−(EF+ε)ℏv(kx−iq)ℏv(kx+iq)−(EF+ε))=0,

which yields

(EF+ε)2−ℏ2v2(kx2+q2)=0⟹kx2+q2=(EF+ε)2ℏ2v2.

For fixed energy ε and fixed transverse momentum q, this is a quadratic equation for kx. It has two roots,

kx=±(EF+ε)2ℏ2v2−q2≡±k,

corresponding to right-moving (+k) and left-moving (−k) waves. This is the physical origin of the incident and reflected waves: the same energy and transverse momentum support both propagation directions.

The Propagation Angle ​

It is convenient to package q and k into a single propagation angle α measured from the normal to the interface. Define

sin⁡α=ℏvqEF+ε,k=EF+εℏvcos⁡α.

Why α is restricted to (−π/2,π/2). The energy EF+ε is the positive definite magnitude of the electron's relativistic momentum in the normal region; it fixes the radius of the constant-energy circle kx2+q2=(EF+ε)2/(ℏv)2. Parametrizing this circle by a polar angle α gives

q=EF+εℏvsin⁡α,kx=±EF+εℏvcos⁡α.

The sign choice in kx is already encoded in ±k; once we fix k>0 and write the spatial factor as e±ikx, the remaining angle α only needs to tell us where on the circle the transverse momentum q sits. Any value outside (−π/2,π/2) would simply duplicate a direction already described by reflecting α→π−α and swapping the sign of kx. Restricting α to the principal branch (−π/2,π/2) therefore labels every distinct propagation direction exactly once, with cos⁡α>0 guaranteeing a real positive longitudinal wave number k.

Extracting the Spinor ​

Return to the explicit matrix equation:

ℏv(0kx−iqkx+iq0)(χ1χ2)=(EF+ε)(χ1χ2).

The two rows give the same ratio. From the first component,

(1)χ2χ1=EF+εℏv(kx−iq).

Now use the dispersion relation kx2+q2=(EF+ε)2/(ℏv)2 to introduce the polar angle α:

kx=EF+εℏvcos⁡α,q=EF+εℏvsin⁡α.

Hence

kx−iq=EF+εℏv(cos⁡α−isin⁡α)=EF+εℏve−iα.

Substituting this into Eq. (1) gives immediately

χ2χ1=eiα.

No trial prefactors, no "Wait — this is not yet a pure phase." The ratio is a pure phase from the start because the magnitude |kx−iq|=(EF+ε)/(ℏv) exactly cancels the numerator.

A convenient symmetric choice is χ1=e−iα/2, χ2=eiα/2, giving the unnormalized spinor

χ+=(e−iα/2eiα/2)(right-moving, kx=+k).

For the left-moving root kx=−k the same algebra with −k−iq=−|k|e−iα yields χ2/χ1=−e−iα. Choosing the symmetric form gives

χ−=(eiα/2−e−iα/2)(left-moving, kx=−k).

Probability-Current Normalization ​

For the Dirac Hamiltonian the probability-current operator is vσx. Its expectation value in state χ is

jx=vχ†σxχ=v(χ1∗χ2+χ2∗χ1).

For the right-mover,

jx(χ+)=v(eiα+e−iα)=2vcos⁡α.

For the left-mover,

jx(χ−)=v(−e−iα−eiα)=−2vcos⁡α.

The sign is opposite, as required: the left-mover carries current in the −x direction. Normalizing to unit probability current (|jx|=1) requires an overall factor 1/2vcos⁡α. Adopting natural units v=1 and incorporating the factor of 2 that arises when passing to the four-component Nambu basis used below, the prefactor becomes 1/cos⁡α. The normalized four-component electron spinors are therefore

Ψe±=eiqy±ikxcos⁡α(e∓iα/2±e±iα/200)

with Ψe− the incident wave (propagating toward the interface at x=0) and Ψe+ the reflected wave (propagating away). The spatial factor e±ikx and the spinor sign ± are correlated so that the reflected state carries current in the opposite x-direction while keeping the same transverse momentum q.

Hole States ​

For hole states (u=0) the lower block of the DBdG equation gives

(EF−p⋅σ)v=εv⟹p⋅σv=(EF−ε)v.

Compared with the electron equation p⋅σu=(EF+ε)u, the only difference is the sign in front of ε: the hole propagates at energy EF−ε rather than EF+ε. The same substitution procedure yields the dispersion relation

kx2+q2=(EF−ε)2ℏ2v2,

and, introducing the hole propagation angle α′ through

sin⁡α′=ℏvqε−EF,k′=ε−EFℏvcos⁡α′,

with α′∈(−π/2,π/2) as before. The crucial point is that the denominator ε−EF is an algebraic quantity. When ε<EF (retro-reflection regime) the denominator is negative, so α′ carries the opposite sign from α for the same transverse momentum q; when ε>EF (specular regime) the denominator is positive and α′ has the same sign as α. The longitudinal wave number k′=(ε−EF)cos⁡α′/ℏv inherits the same sign as ε−EF, so k′ is negative in the retro regime and positive in the specular regime.

the eigen spinors are

Ψh±=eiqy±ik′x1cos⁡α′(00e∓iα′/2∓e±iα′/2).

The critical observation is the sign of (EF−ε). When ε<EF, the hole energy EF−ε lies above the Dirac point; the hole state therefore sits on the conduction-band side of the spectrum, where its group velocity points opposite to its wave vector (retro-reflection). When ε>EF, the hole energy lies below the Dirac point; the state sits on the valence-band side, where the group velocity points parallel to the wave vector (specular reflection). The relative orientation of group velocity and wave vector is opposite in the two cases, and this single fact controls the reflection geometry.

5. The Critical Angle ​

The angle α′ is real only when |sin⁡α′|≤1, which defines a critical angle

αc=arcsin(|EF−ε|EF+ε).

For |α|<αc, Andreev reflection is kinematically allowed and four scattering states coexist in the normal region: Ψe− (incident electron), Ψe+ (normal reflection), Ψh− (incident hole), and Ψh+ (Andreev-reflected hole). For |α|>αc, the hole cannot propagate and Andreev reflection is suppressed; only normal reflection survives.

6. Superconducting Region and the Heavily Doped Limit ​

In the superconducting region (x<0) the potential is U=−U0 and the pairing potential is Δ=Δ0eiϕ. The DBdG equation reads

(ℏvk⋅σ−U0−EFΔ0eiϕΔ0e−iϕU0+EF−ℏvk⋅σ)(uv)=ε(uv).

It is convenient to define the superconducting Fermi energy ES0=U0+EF and the normal-state kinetic energy measured from that Fermi level,

ξk=ℏv|k|−ES0.

Deriving the Superconducting Dispersion ​

Write the superconducting DBdG Hamiltonian in compact block form. With h=ℏvk⋅σ and ES0=U0+EF,

H=(h−ES0Δ0eiϕ12Δ0e−iϕ12ES0−h)≡(h−ES0ΔΔ†ES0−h),

where Δ=Δ0eiϕ12 is proportional to the identity in sublattice space because the proximity-induced s-wave pairing does not mix A and B sites.

Step 1: Square the matrix ​

H2=((h−ES0)2+|Δ|2[h,Δ][Δ†,h](ES0−h)2+|Δ|2).

Because Δ is a scalar multiple of the identity, it commutes with everything: [h,Δ]=0. The off-diagonal blocks vanish.

Step 2: Diagonalize h in sublattice space ​

The kinetic block is h=ℏvk⋅σ=ℏv|k|n^⋅σ with n^=k/|k|. Using the Pauli anticommutator {σi,σj}=2δij,

h2=(ℏv|k|)2(n^⋅σ)2=(ℏv|k|)212.

Thus h has eigenvalues ±ℏv|k|. The corresponding eigen spinors are the chiral states χ± satisfying (n^⋅σ)χ±=±χ±. In this chiral basis h is diagonal:

h→diag(+ℏv|k|,−ℏv|k|).

Step 3: Evaluate the diagonal blocks in the chiral basis ​

With h diagonal, each 2×2 block of H2 becomes diagonal too. The upper-left block is

(h−ES0)2+Δ02→diag[(ℏv|k|−ES0)2+Δ02,(−ℏv|k|−ES0)2+Δ02].

The lower-right block gives the same two eigenvalues. The four eigenvalues of H2 are therefore

ε2=(±ℏv|k|−ES0)2+Δ02,(each doubly degenerate).

Step 4: Heavy-doping (Andreev) limit ​

In the heavily doped superconductor ES0≫ℏv|k|,Δ0,ε. Two of the four roots correspond to energies far from the Fermi level:

ε≈±(ES0+ℏv|k|)(high-energy, frozen modes).

The remaining two roots live near the Fermi surface. Defining the normal-state kinetic energy

ξk=ℏv|k|−ES0,

these low-energy modes satisfy

ε2=ξk2+Δ02=(ℏv|k|−ES0)2+Δ02.

This is the familiar BCS gapped spectrum, with the crucial difference that ξk is linear in |k| rather than quadratic. The heavy-doping limit has projected the four-component Dirac–Bogoliubov problem onto the single pair of Nambu states that couple to the subgap excitations in the normal region.

This is the familiar gapped spectrum of a BCS superconductor, except that the normal-state dispersion ξk is linear in |k| rather than quadratic.

Subgap States and Exponential Decay ​

For excitation energies below the gap, |ε|<Δ0, the dispersion relation gives

(ℏv|k|−ES0)2=ε2−Δ02<0.

The right-hand side is negative, so ℏv|k|−ES0 must be purely imaginary. Writing

ℏv|k|−ES0=±iΔ02−ε2,

the wave number acquires an imaginary part

Im(|k|)=Δ02−ε2ℏv≡κ.

The wave function therefore decays exponentially into the superconductor as e−κ|x|. There are no propagating single-particle modes in the subgap regime; all incident probability current from the normal side must be reflected.

The Heavily Doped Limit ​

When the superconducting electrode is heavily doped, U0≫EF,ε,Δ0, the superconducting Fermi energy ES0 is very large. The decay constant can be written in a compact form by parameterizing the energy as ε=Δ0cos⁡β with β∈(0,π). Then

κ=Δ02−ε2ℏv=Δ0sin⁡βℏv.

This expression enters the boundary-condition matching through the ratios of decaying exponentials at x=0.

7. Boundary Matching ​

Superconducting States in the Heavily Doped Limit ​

In the superconducting region (x<0) the subgap solutions decay exponentially as x→−∞. In the heavily doped limit U0≫EF,ε,Δ0 there are exactly two such decaying modes, distinguished by the sign of the large real part of the longitudinal wave number (±k0 with k0≈ES0/ℏv). Their four-component spinors at x=0 are

ΨS+(0)=(e−iβe−iβe−iϕe−iϕ),ΨS−(0)=(eiβ−eiβe−iϕ−e−iϕ).

The derivation proceeds from the DBdG equation in the superconductor by noting that the kinetic term ℏvkxσx dominates and must be balanced against the large potential ES0. For the +k0 branch the electron and hole spinors both lie in the σx=+1 eigenspace (1,1)T; for the −k0 branch they lie in the σx=−1 eigenspace (1,−1)T. Solving the remaining 2×2 electron-hole block to first order in Δ0/ES0 yields the ratios v/u=e−i(ϕ−β) for ΨS+ and v/u=e−i(ϕ+β) for ΨS−. The common phase e−iβ in the upper two components of ΨS+ (and eiβ in ΨS−) is a convenient choice of overall gauge.

The Scattering State in the Normal Region ​

An electron is incident from the normal side (x>0) toward the interface. The scattering state is a superposition of the incident electron, a normally reflected electron, and an Andreev-reflected hole:

ΨN(x,y)=Ψe−(x,y)+rΨe+(x,y)+rAΨh+(x,y).

The amplitudes r (normal reflection) and rA (Andreev reflection) are the quantities we seek. Evaluated at the interface (x=0), the three normal-region spinors are

Ψe−(0)=1cos⁡α(eiα/2−e−iα/200),Ψe+(0)=1cos⁡α(e−iα/2eiα/200),Ψh+(0)=1cos⁡α′(00e−iα′/2−eiα′/2).

Continuity at the Interface ​

Demanding continuity of the full four-component wave function at x=0 gives

Ψe−(0)+rΨe+(0)+rAΨh+(0)=aΨS+(0)+bΨS−(0).

Writing this out component by component:

Component 1 (A sublattice, electron):

(1)eiα/2+re−iα/2cos⁡α=ae−iβ+beiβ.

Component 2 (B sublattice, electron):

(2)−e−iα/2+reiα/2cos⁡α=ae−iβ−beiβ.

Component 3 (A sublattice, hole):

(3)rAe−iα′/2cos⁡α′=(a+b)e−iϕ.

Component 4 (B sublattice, hole):

(4)−rAeiα′/2cos⁡α′=(a−b)e−iϕ.

Solving for the Superconducting Amplitudes ​

Equations (3) and (4) involve only the hole sector and can be solved immediately. Adding and subtracting them gives

a=−irAeiϕsin⁡(α′/2)cos⁡α′,b=rAeiϕcos⁡(α′/2)cos⁡α′.

Solving for the Reflection Amplitudes ​

Insert these expressions for a and b into equations (1) and (2). Adding (1) and (2) eliminates b:

(5)isin⁡(α/2)+rcos⁡(α/2)cos⁡α=−irAeiϕsin⁡(α′/2)e−iβcos⁡α′.

Subtracting (2) from (1) eliminates a:

(6)cos⁡(α/2)−irsin⁡(α/2)cos⁡α=rAeiϕcos⁡(α′/2)eiβcos⁡α′.

Equations (5) and (6) are two linear equations for the two unknowns r and rA. It is convenient to eliminate rA first. Multiply (5) by cos⁡(α′/2)eiβ and (6) by isin⁡(α′/2)e−iβ, then add the results. After simplifying the trigonometric products with the identities

sin⁡α′2cos⁡α2=12[sin⁡α′+α2+sin⁡α′−α2],cos⁡α′2sin⁡α2=12[sin⁡α′+α2−sin⁡α′−α2],

and similarly for the cosine products, one finds that the left-hand side combines into a single denominator

X=cos⁡βcos⁡α′−α2+isin⁡βcos⁡α′+α2,

while the right-hand side yields the Andreev amplitude

rA=e−iϕX−1cos⁡αcos⁡α′(|α|<αc).

Once rA is known, r follows by substituting back into either (5) or (6). The result is

r=X−1[−cos⁡βsin⁡α′+α2+isin⁡βsin⁡α′−α2].

Unitarity ​

For subgap energies |ε|<Δ0 the parameter β=arccos⁡(ε/Δ0) is real, and one may verify directly that

|r|2+|rA|2=cos2⁡βsin2⁡α′+α2+sin2⁡βsin2⁡α′−α2+cos⁡αcos⁡α′|X|2=1.

The equality follows from the trigonometric identity

|X|2=12[1+cos⁡αcos⁡α′+sin⁡αsin⁡α′cos⁡(2β)],

combined with the half-angle formulas for the sine squares in the numerator. Since no propagating modes exist in the superconductor below the gap, all probability current must be reflected; the reflection matrix is therefore unitary, with |r|2 the probability for normal reflection and |rA|2 the probability for Andreev reflection.

8. Retro-Reflection versus Specular Reflection ​

The distinction between the two regimes hinges on the relative sign of α and α′.

The Retro-Reflective Regime (EF≫ε) ​

When the normal region is heavily doped, the Fermi energy sits deep in the conduction band. To leading order in ε/EF,

sin⁡α′≈ℏvqEF≈sin⁡α⇒α′≈−α.

The hole lies in the conduction band, so its group velocity points opposite to its wave vector. The reflected hole therefore retraces the incident electron trajectory. This is the conventional retro-reflection familiar from non-relativistic metals.

In this limit the amplitudes simplify to

rA=e−iϕcos⁡α(ε/Δ0)cos⁡α+ζ,r=−ζsin⁡α(ε/Δ0)cos⁡α+ζ,

with ζ=1−(ε/Δ0)2.

The Specular Regime (EF≪ε) ​

At low doping, or equivalently for injection energies well above the Fermi level, the hole resides in the valence band. Now

sin⁡α′≈ℏvqε≈sin⁡α⇒α′≈α.

The hole group velocity points in the same direction as its wave vector. The reflected hole therefore exits the interface at the same angle as the incident electron, on the same side of the normal — a mirror-like specular reflection.

The amplitudes in this limit become

rA=e−iϕcos⁡αε/Δ0+ζcos⁡α,r=−(ε/Δ0)sin⁡αε/Δ0+ζcos⁡α.

The transition from retro- to specular reflection is not merely a change in trajectory geometry; it corresponds to a qualitative shift in the voltage dependence of the subgap conductance.

9. Perfect Andreev Reflection at Normal Incidence ​

At normal incidence (α=0, q=0) the expressions collapse to their simplest form. The normal reflection amplitude vanishes identically,

r=0,

while the Andreev reflection amplitude becomes a pure phase factor,

rA=e−i(ϕ+β).

The reflection probability is therefore |rA|2=1 and |r|2=0: every incident electron is converted into a reflected hole with unit efficiency.

This result is remarkable because the Fermi wavelength mismatch between the normal and superconducting regions is enormous — in the heavily doped superconductor the Fermi momentum is orders of magnitude larger than in graphene. In a conventional metal such a mismatch would suppress Andreev reflection through normal reflection. The perfect conversion in graphene is protected by pseudospin (chirality) conservation: the incident electron, the reflected hole, and the transmitted Cooper pair all share the same sublattice pseudospin at α=0, while normal reflection would require a chirality flip and is therefore forbidden.

The analogy with Klein tunneling is direct. A massless Dirac fermion incident normally on a potential barrier transmits with probability T=1 because chirality cannot be flipped by a scalar potential. At an NS interface the same symmetry ensures perfect electron-to-hole conversion.

10. Conductance and the Inversion of Voltage Dependence ​

The differential conductance of the NS interface at subgap voltages is proportional to the angle-integrated Andreev reflection probability. In the two limiting regimes this integral yields qualitatively different behaviors.

For retro-reflection (EF≫ε), the conductance increases with applied voltage. The phase space for Andreev reflection grows as ε approaches Δ0, and the interface becomes increasingly transparent to Cooper-pair injection.

For specular reflection (EF≪ε), the opposite occurs: the conductance decreases with voltage. The reason is that in the specular regime the hole propagates on the opposite side of the charge-neutrality point from the electron. The angular dependence of the reflection amplitude suppresses the integrated probability as ε increases, because the condition |α|<αc becomes progressively more restrictive.

The crossover between these two behaviors occurs when the Fermi energy is tuned through the Dirac point. Experimentally, this means that sweeping the gate voltage (which controls EF) across charge neutrality should produce an inversion of the subgap conductance curve: from a rising conductance characteristic of retro-reflection to a falling conductance characteristic of specular reflection.

11. Experimental Outlook ​

The observation of specular Andreev reflection requires graphene samples in which the Fermi energy can be tuned close to the Dirac point while maintaining good electrical contact to a superconducting electrode. Early experiments on graphene–aluminum junctions confirmed the predicted retro-reflective behavior at high doping. The specular regime remains more challenging because disorder and charge inhomogeneity broaden the Dirac point, washing out the sharp crossover.

More recent advances in van der Waals heterostructures — in particular, encapsulating graphene in hexagonal boron nitride — have reduced disorder to levels where the Dirac point is resolved with sub-meV precision. Combined with superconducting contacts made from NbSe2 or other 2D superconductors, these devices offer a realistic platform for observing the full transition from retro- to specular reflection and the associated conductance inversion.

The broader significance extends beyond graphene itself. Any Dirac material — topological insulator surface states, three-dimensional Dirac semimetals, or moiré superlattices — hosts massless charge carriers whose chirality fundamentally alters the Andreev reflection kinematics. Specular reflection is not an exotic curiosity but a generic consequence of linear dispersion married to superconducting proximity.

References ​

  • C. W. J. Beenakker, "Specular Andreev reflection in graphene," Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 067007 (2006), arXiv:cond-mat/0604594.
